verte.title: Verte
verte.subtitle: Nowhere's End Absinthe / Verte
verte.intro: A classic green absinthe: bitter, aromatic, and bright with a clean, dry finish.
verte.story: Verte is the most traditional expression in the line, built around wormwood, anise, fennel, and alpine herbs for a vivid green louche and a focused aperitif ritual.
verte.effectTerritory: Bright, bitter, and lucidly herbal; built for a focused aperitif ritual.
verte.serve: Serve 1:4 with cold water, no sugar or half sugar.
verte.flavor: Bitter green, classic louche, melissa and coriander lift.
verte.marketingPhrase: For the green hour before dinner.

recipeSections.title: Primary botanical bill
items: Classic bitter backbone and absinthe identity.
Extends the bitter line with softer Artemisia nuance.
Carries the sweet anisic body and the louche.
Rounds the body and keeps the sweetness botanical.
Gives green citrus lift and aroma precision.
Acts as a root fixative and drying frame.
Introduces perceived sharpness without reading toothpaste.

recipeSections.title: Claim-safe positioning
items: Use ritual language rather than saying stimulant, focus aid, or nootropic.
Aperitif and aromatic clarity are defensible; drug-effect language is not.
Do not imply enhanced cognition or stimulant function.

recipeSections.title: Extraction plan
items: Run grand wormwood, Roman wormwood, green anise, fennel, coriander, and angelica in the still for the structural absinthe spine.
Use hyssop and lemon balm in a short controlled post-distillation maceration to set the verte color and green aromatic lift without stewing the batch.
Hold gentian and mint to restrained cold extraction or tincture dosing so bitterness and cooling stay precise rather than muddy.

herbs: extends the wormwood line with softer nuance
sweet aromatic spine and louche support
fresh citron lift without breaking the style
resinous green top notes and medicinal altitude
molecules: sweet anisic body and louche behavior
dry fennel tension that keeps the sweetness trimmed
small floral lift that broadens the mid-palate
a tiny cooling edge in the finish
serviceNotes: Talk about aperitif structure, attention to ritual, and green bitterness, not cognition claims.
The bottle should feel classically absinthe even when its clarity cues are modernized.

rouge.title: Crimson
rouge.subtitle: Nowhere's End Absinthe / Crimson
rouge.intro: A crimson absinthe with floral tartness, warm spice, and a dramatic ruby pour.
rouge.story: Crimson keeps the absinthe spine intact while opening into hibiscus, rosehip, citrus, and rose for a vivid, table-centered serve.
rouge.effectTerritory: Tart, floral, crimson, and convivial; designed for a dramatic table ritual.
rouge.serve: Serve 1:3.5 with cold water and optional expressed orange peel.
rouge.flavor: Hibiscus, citrus, anise, warm spice, and floral bitter finish.
rouge.marketingPhrase: A crimson absinthe for the table.

recipeSections.title: Primary botanical bill
items: Creates a citrus bridge into hibiscus.
Lifts the top note with sparkling spice.

recipeSections.title: Claim-safe positioning
items: Use convivial and table-centered language rather than mood or heart-health claims.
Frame the bottle as a dramatic ritual for the table.
Do not imply cardiovascular or sexual-function benefits.

recipeSections.title: Extraction plan
items: Distill wormwoods, anise, fennel, star anise, coriander, orange peel, angelica, cardamom, and grains of paradise for a stable absinthe frame.
Do not drive hibiscus or rosehip through a hot still maceration; they are better handled outside the main distillation path to preserve fresh tart color.
Build the crimson layer with controlled cold extracts or finishing tinctures of hibiscus, rosehip, and trace rose petal, then bench the dose against louche strength and acid balance.

herbs: keeps the red build anchored to absinthe
citrus bridge to the red floral core
molecules: stops the profile from becoming plush
adds rose bloom and soft lift
serviceNotes: Position it as a vivid table ritual, not a physiological mood modifier.
The visual story should support conviviality without drifting into supplement language.

detour.title: Detour
detour.subtitle: Nowhere's End Absinthe / Detour
detour.intro: A less-licorice absinthe with citrus, resin, and a blue-to-violet ritual in the glass.
detour.story: Detour steps away from the expected path, leaning into citrus peel, juniper, mastic, and butterfly pea for an absinthe that turns as it opens.
detour.effectTerritory: A less-licorice absinthe detour: citrus, resin, blue-to-violet ritual, and botanical lift.
detour.serve: Serve 1:4 with cold water; a citrus-acid drop can trigger the blue-to-violet shift.
detour.flavor: Less licorice, more citrus, juniper, resin, and botanical lift.

recipeSections.title: Primary botanical bill
items: Keeps the expression dry, adult, and bitter.
Adds louche texture without pushing the anise line too hard.
Pushes the top note toward clean citrus brightness.
Gives resinous mouthfeel and ritual strangeness.

recipeSections.title: Claim-safe positioning
items: Use unexpected and turning language rather than cognitive or psychedelic claims.
Make the ritual itself the point.
Do not imply altered consciousness benefits.

recipeSections.title: Extraction plan
items: Run wormwoods, low anise, fennel, coriander, lemon peel, grapefruit peel, juniper, angelica, and trace rosemary in the still to establish the dry citrus-resin base.
If mastic is used as a resinous bridge, keep it to a very light post-distillation maceration or tincture addition so it reads as texture rather than turpentine.
Keep butterfly pea and cornflower entirely out of the still and build the blue-violet ritual through cold extraction or separate finishing components.

herbs: keeps the expression dry, adult, and bitter
molecules: keeps the citrus line bright and green
serviceNotes: Sell the visual transformation and the side-road feeling, not altered consciousness.
Detour should read as unexpected but still dry and adult.

bleue.title: Bleue
bleue.subtitle: Nowhere's End Absinthe / Bleue
bleue.intro: A clear absinthe with an alpine frame, a slow louche, and a cool, quiet finish.
bleue.story: Bleue is the clearest and most restrained expression in the line, built around fennel, wormwood, angelica, and alpine herbs for a slow, contemplative serve.
bleue.effectTerritory: Cool, clear, and alpine; a quiet, contemplative absinthe.
bleue.serve: Serve 1:4.5 with very cold water and a slow drip.
bleue.flavor: Clear, fennel-soft, angelica, alpine herb, and mint trace.

recipeSections.title: Primary botanical bill
items: Makes the bottle soft and rounded.
Keeps the structure bright but not sharp.

recipeSections.title: Claim-safe positioning
items: Use quiet and contemplative rather than anxiety or sleep language.
The calm comes from serve style and aromatic posture.

recipeSections.title: Extraction plan
items: Distill wormwood, Roman wormwood or genepi, fennel, anise, angelica, coriander, and restrained hyssop for the quiet alpine frame.
Avoid green coloration maceration if the bottle is meant to stay bleue-clear; any botanical resting step should be short and color-neutral.
Use mint, elderflower, or cornflower only as tiny cold-extract or tincture adjustments if needed, keeping the final profile clear and spare.

molecules: holds the louche and sweet spice line
serviceNotes: Describe this as a contemplative pour, not a relaxant or sleep aid.
Its calm should be sensory and ritual, not medicinal.
